questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate word from the options given below to complete the following sentence.

Given the seriousness of the situation that he had to face, his ___ was impressive.

A. beggary  B. nomenclature  C. jealousy  D. nonchalance
questions.to_find: most appropriate word to fill the blank expressing calmness under pressure

understand.plain: The sentence praises someone who faced a very serious situation yet remained outwardly calm. The blank needs a noun that describes impressive composure or cool-headedness. Beggary means extreme poverty, nomenclature is a naming system, and jealousy is envy — none of these fit. Nonchalance means a relaxed, unconcerned manner that others admire.

keywords.explain: A calm, casual attitude showing no anxiety even under difficult circumstances
keywords.example: She accepted the bad news with remarkable nonchalance, never losing her smile.

keywords.explain: The set of names or terms used in a particular field or system
keywords.example: The nomenclature of chemical compounds follows IUPAC rules.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a vocabulary completion question.

given.aim: Select the word that best completes the sentence about composure under a serious situation

terms.meaning: The gravity or severity of the circumstances faced
terms.example: A crisis at work highlights the seriousness of decision-making.
terms.connects: Sets the context: something difficult or grave is happening.

terms.meaning: Evoking admiration; notably effective or striking
terms.example: Her impressive performance earned a standing ovation.
terms.connects: The blank must be something positive and admirable, ruling out negative traits.

terms.meaning: Relaxed composure; absence of worry or anxiety in the face of difficulty
terms.example: His nonchalance during the earthquake surprised everyone.
terms.connects: Matches both the adversity (serious situation) and the positive framing (impressive).

given.plan: Eliminate options that are negative or irrelevant to 'calmness'; identify the word that collocates naturally with 'impressive' in an adversarial context.

steps.title: Check each option for meaning fit
steps.apply: beggary = extreme poverty (not relevant); nomenclature = naming system (not relevant); jealousy = envy (negative trait); nonchalance = composed calmness (positive, admirable).
steps.note: Three options are either negative or unrelated to the context of personal composure.

steps.title: Verify collocational correctness
steps.apply: 'His nonchalance was impressive' reads naturally. Nonchalance under serious adversity is indeed impressive — it signals inner strength and self-control.
steps.note: The sentence structure 'his ___ was impressive' needs a personal quality noun; nonchalance fits perfectly.

questions.formula_note: This is a vocabulary question; no mathematical formula is required.

questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate word from the options given below to complete the following sentence.

Friendship, no matter how _________ it is, has its limitations.

A. cordial  B. intimate  C. secret  D. pleasant
questions.to_find: most appropriate word to describe the depth of friendship while acknowledging its limitations

understand.plain: The sentence makes a universal claim that even the deepest friendship has its limits. The blank needs a word that expresses the strongest possible degree of friendship, so that the contrast with 'limitations' is most meaningful. Intimate means very close and personal, representing the highest depth of a friendship.

keywords.explain: Very close, deeply personal; the strongest descriptor of closeness in a relationship
keywords.example: They were intimate friends who shared their deepest secrets with each other.

keywords.explain: Warm and friendly, but not necessarily deeply close
keywords.example: The two colleagues maintained a cordial relationship despite their differences.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a vocabulary completion question.

given.aim: Identify the word that conveys the highest degree of closeness in a friendship

terms.meaning: Regardless of the degree or intensity expressed by the adjective
terms.example: 'No matter how strong the team is, they can lose.' — signals maximum degree.
terms.connects: Points to needing the extreme adjective that makes the 'still has limits' contrast most powerful.

terms.meaning: Restrictions, bounds, or constraints that apply even to positive things
terms.example: Even the best tools have their limitations in extreme conditions.
terms.connects: The contrast word — even at maximum closeness, there are still limits.

terms.meaning: Characterized by close personal acquaintance and familiarity; deeply personal
terms.example: An intimate friendship involves sharing one's inner thoughts freely.
terms.connects: Represents the greatest depth of friendship, making the concessive clause most meaningful.

given.plan: Use the 'no matter how ... still has limits' pattern to identify which adjective best expresses the strongest degree of friendship closeness.

steps.title: Analyse the concessive structure
steps.apply: The phrase 'no matter how ___ it is, has its limitations' uses a concessive clause. It argues that even at the maximum value of the blank quality, the friendship still has limits. This structure favours the word expressing the greatest depth.
steps.note: Cordial and pleasant are mild descriptors; secret describes a property, not closeness depth.

steps.title: Rank the options by degree of closeness
steps.apply: pleasant < cordial < intimate in terms of closeness; secret is not a measure of closeness at all. Intimate is the superlative of friendship depth among the given choices.
steps.note: B. intimate is the correct answer.

questions.formula_note: This is a vocabulary question; no mathematical formula is required.

questions.question:
Complete the sentence.

Dare _______________ mistakes.

A. commit  B. to commit  C. committed  D. committing
questions.to_find: grammatically correct verb form after 'dare' used as a modal

understand.plain: The key is recognising that 'dare' here is used as a semi-modal (bare modal) auxiliary verb. When 'dare' acts as a modal, it is followed by the bare infinitive (verb without 'to'). The sentence means 'Have the boldness to make mistakes,' a motivational instruction often used in GATE aptitude questions.

keywords.explain: The base form of a verb without 'to', used after modal auxiliaries like can, must, dare (modal use)
keywords.example: 'You dare not speak' — 'speak' is the bare infinitive after the modal 'dare'.

keywords.explain: Dare can work as a full verb (dare + to-infinitive) or as a modal auxiliary (dare + bare infinitive); in modal use, no 'to' is needed
keywords.example: 'Dare commit errors' = modal dare; 'He dares to commit errors' = full verb dare.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a grammar completion question.

given.aim: Determine the correct form of the verb that follows 'dare' in this sentence

terms.meaning: When used as an auxiliary, 'dare' is followed by the bare infinitive with no 'to'
terms.example: 'Dare challenge the norm!' — challenge is the bare infinitive.
terms.connects: Tells us to select the base verb form without any auxiliary or tense marking.

terms.meaning: The uninflected base form of the verb meaning to do or carry out
terms.example: 'Commit yourself fully to the task.'
terms.connects: This is the bare infinitive that follows the modal 'dare'.

terms.meaning: The full infinitive used after full (non-modal) verbs
terms.example: 'He dares to commit mistakes' uses dare as a full verb.
terms.connects: Would be correct only if 'dare' were used as a full lexical verb with a subject; here it is modal.

given.plan: Identify whether 'dare' is modal or full verb, then apply the correct infinitive form.

steps.title: Identify the role of 'dare'
steps.apply: The sentence 'Dare ___ mistakes' has no explicit subject and uses 'dare' as an imperative/modal form. In this modal use, the following verb takes the bare infinitive.
steps.note: 'Committed' and 'committing' are past/present participles, incorrect after a modal.

steps.title: Select the bare infinitive
steps.apply: Bare infinitive = commit (option A). Option B 'to commit' is for full-verb dare usage. The completed sentence 'Dare commit mistakes' is a valid idiomatic English imperative meaning 'Be bold enough to make mistakes'.

questions.formula_note: This is a grammar question; no mathematical formula is required.

questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate word from the options given below to complete the following sentence.

One of his biggest ______ was his ability to forgive.

A. vice  B. virtues  C. choices  D. strength
questions.to_find: most appropriate word that fits 'ability to forgive' as a moral quality

understand.plain: The sentence describes a positive moral quality — the ability to forgive. The blank needs a word that denotes positive moral traits. 'Virtue' means a morally good quality or habit, which is precisely what the ability to forgive represents. 'Vice' is the opposite (a moral failing), 'choices' is too general, and 'strength' (while possible) doesn't grammatically pair as naturally with 'one of his biggest'.

keywords.explain: A positive moral quality or habit; traits like courage, honesty, and forgiveness are virtues
keywords.example: Patience is a virtue that helps one deal with difficult situations calmly.

keywords.explain: An immoral or wicked behaviour or habit; the opposite of virtue
keywords.example: Greed is a vice that can corrupt even good-natured people.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a vocabulary question.

given.aim: Identify the word that classifies 'the ability to forgive' as a positive moral attribute

terms.meaning: The capacity to pardon others for wrongs done; a positively regarded moral trait
terms.example: Nelson Mandela's ability to forgive his captors became legendary.
terms.connects: Establishes the blank must be a positive moral quality category.

terms.meaning: Plural of virtue — moral excellences or admirable qualities in character
terms.example: Honesty, kindness, and forgiveness are virtues taught in most cultures.
terms.connects: Forgiveness is classically listed among virtues in ethics.

terms.meaning: Most significant or greatest in impact
terms.example: Her biggest strength was her empathy for others.
terms.connects: Signals the item is notable and should be framed as a positive attribute.

given.plan: Eliminate the negative option (vice) and generics (choices, strength which doesn't collocate as well), leaving virtues as the correct moral category.

steps.title: Eliminate incorrect options
steps.apply: Vice is incorrect because 'ability to forgive' is admirable, not a moral failing. Choices is too neutral and vague. Strength is singular and collocation 'one of his biggest strength' is grammatically incorrect (should be 'strengths').
steps.note: Grammatical agreement: 'one of his biggest ___' requires the plural form; virtues is plural, strengths would be correct but is not an option.

steps.title: Confirm virtues as correct
steps.apply: 'One of his biggest virtues was his ability to forgive' is both grammatically correct (plural 'virtues' after 'one of') and semantically accurate (forgiveness is a classic virtue).

questions.formula_note: This is a vocabulary question; no mathematical formula is required.

questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate word from the options given below to complete the following sentence.

A person suffering from Alzheimer's disease ______ short-term memory loss.

A. Experienced  B. Has experienced  C. Is experiencing  D. Experiences
questions.to_find: correct verb tense to describe a permanent or habitual symptom of Alzheimer's disease

understand.plain: Alzheimer's disease is a chronic, ongoing condition. When describing a permanent symptom or characteristic of a disease (or a person currently afflicted with it), English uses the simple present tense to express a habitual or permanent truth. 'Experiences' (simple present) is the correct choice because it expresses what someone with the disease characteristically undergoes, not a past event or an ongoing temporary action.

keywords.explain: Used for permanent states, general truths, habits, or characteristics that are always true
keywords.example: 'Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius' — a permanent truth in simple present.

keywords.explain: Used for actions happening right now or temporary activities; not suitable for permanent conditions
keywords.example: 'She is experiencing headaches this week' — temporary, currently happening.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a grammar and tense question.

given.aim: Select the verb form that correctly describes an ongoing, permanent medical symptom

terms.meaning: A progressive, irreversible neurological disorder with permanent ongoing symptoms
terms.example: A patient with Alzheimer's disease requires full-time care as the disease progresses.
terms.connects: Signals that the condition is permanent and habitual, not past or temporary.

terms.meaning: Inability to retain new information for short periods; a hallmark symptom of Alzheimer's
terms.example: Short-term memory loss makes it difficult to remember conversations from minutes ago.
terms.connects: This is a characteristic symptom — always present in the affected person.

terms.meaning: Verb form expressing habitual truths and permanent states
terms.example: 'A diabetic patient experiences frequent thirst' — permanent characteristic.
terms.connects: Best tense to state a permanent, defining symptom of a chronic disease.

given.plan: Analyse whether the symptom is past, temporary current, or permanent; select the tense matching a permanent characteristic.

steps.title: Classify the type of statement
steps.apply: The sentence makes a general statement about what someone with Alzheimer's disease characteristically undergoes. This is a permanent truth about the disease, not a past event (rules out A) or a temporary ongoing action (rules out C). 'Has experienced' (B) implies completion, which conflicts with the ongoing nature of the disease.
steps.note: Permanent truths and habitual characteristics use simple present in English.

steps.title: Select the simple present form
steps.apply: 'A person suffering from Alzheimer's disease experiences short-term memory loss.' This uses simple present to state a defining symptom — exactly the correct usage.

questions.formula_note: This is a grammar tense question; no mathematical formula is required.

questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate word from the options given below to complete the following sentence.

_______ is the key to their happiness; they are satisfied with what they have.

A. Contentment  B. Ambition  C. Perseverance  D. Hunger
questions.to_find: word that names the state of being satisfied with what one has

understand.plain: The second clause 'they are satisfied with what they have' is a direct explanation of the first clause. The blank must name exactly that quality — being satisfied with one's current situation. Contentment means peaceful happiness and satisfaction with what one has, which perfectly matches the explanation given. Ambition, perseverance, and hunger all imply striving for more, the opposite of the given satisfaction.

keywords.explain: A state of happiness and satisfaction with what one already possesses
keywords.example: After years of chasing success, she found contentment in simple pleasures.

keywords.explain: A strong desire to achieve something more, often implying dissatisfaction with the current state
keywords.example: His ambition drove him to work sixteen hours a day to reach the top.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a vocabulary question.

given.aim: Identify the noun that means 'being satisfied with what one has'

terms.meaning: Finding fulfilment and peace in one's current possessions or situation without craving more
terms.example: A minimalist lifestyle reflects being satisfied with what one has.
terms.connects: This explanation directly defines contentment.

terms.meaning: The central factor or cause that produces happiness
terms.example: Gratitude is often cited as the key to happiness.
terms.connects: The blank is the factor causing the happiness described in the rest of the sentence.

terms.meaning: The quality of being pleased and satisfied; inner peace with one's circumstances
terms.example: Contentment does not require wealth — it requires acceptance.
terms.connects: Directly matches 'satisfied with what they have'.

given.plan: Use the second clause as a direct definition of the blank; match it to the option that means exactly 'satisfied with what one has'.

steps.title: Use the second clause as a definition clue
steps.apply: The sentence structure is: '[Blank] is the key to their happiness; [explanation of blank].' The explanation 'they are satisfied with what they have' defines the blank word. Match: Contentment = satisfaction with one's current state.
steps.note: Ambition (B) = desire for more; Perseverance (C) = persistence through difficulty; Hunger (D) = craving more. All three are antithetical to 'satisfied with what they have'.

steps.title: Confirm contentment is the match
steps.apply: 'Contentment is the key to their happiness; they are satisfied with what they have.' The word and its explanation are synonymous — perfect fit.

questions.formula_note: This is a vocabulary question; no mathematical formula is required.

questions.question:
Choose the most appropriate word from the options given below to complete the following sentence.

Many ancient cultures attributed disease to supernatural causes. However, modern science has largely helped ________ such notions.

A. Impel  B. Dispel  C. Propel  D. Repel
questions.to_find: most appropriate word meaning to drive away or eliminate false beliefs or notions

understand.plain: The sentence contrasts ancient supernatural beliefs with modern scientific understanding. Modern science has driven away or eliminated those false notions. The word needed means 'to cause something (especially a belief or fear) to go away or disappear.' Dispel means exactly that — to make a doubt, fear, or false idea disappear. Impel means to urge forward, propel means to drive something ahead, and repel means to push back/away physically or emotionally.

keywords.explain: To cause a belief, fear, or feeling to end or go away; to drive away doubts or false notions
keywords.example: The teacher dispelled the myth that lightning never strikes the same place twice.

keywords.explain: To drive back physically or to cause disgust; does not collocate with abstract notions in the same idiomatic way
keywords.example: The insect spray repelled mosquitoes effectively.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a vocabulary question.

given.aim: Select the verb that means to eliminate or drive away false ideas or notions

terms.meaning: The previously mentioned ideas — attributing disease to supernatural causes
terms.example: The notion that illness is a curse is a supernatural belief.
terms.connects: Identifies what the verb must act upon: abstract beliefs or false ideas.

terms.meaning: The verb must collocate with 'notions' and mean to eliminate or drive away abstract beliefs
terms.example: 'Science helped dispel the notion that the Earth is flat.'
terms.connects: Idiomatic collocation: 'dispel notions/myths/fears' is standard English.

terms.meaning: To make a mistaken belief or negative feeling disappear or vanish
terms.example: Research has helped dispel long-held misconceptions about vaccines.
terms.connects: Matches both the context (false beliefs) and the standard collocation 'dispel notions'.

given.plan: Check which verb collocates idiomatically with 'notions' in the sense of eliminating or overcoming false beliefs.

steps.title: Analyse the meaning context
steps.apply: Modern science overcame/eliminated supernatural notions. The verb must mean: 'cause abstract beliefs to disappear'. Impel = push someone to act; Propel = drive forward; Repel = push physically back or cause disgust. None of these collocate naturally with 'notions'.
steps.note: Dispel is the standard idiomatic verb used with notions, myths, rumours, and fears.

steps.title: Confirm dispel with collocation test
steps.apply: 'Modern science has helped dispel such notions' — this is a standard English construction. 'Dispel a notion/myth/fear/doubt' is a well-established collocation.

questions.formula_note: This is a vocabulary question; no mathematical formula is required.
